abstract: Developing alkali metal-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with permanent porosity remains a significant challenge due to the high coordination affinity of alkali ions for solvating molecules like water. Despite this difficulty, such materials offer distinct gravimetric advantages for gas adsorption applications due to their lightweight nature. Herein, we report the synthesis of a novel sodium-based microporous MOF, HIAM-111, assembled from an octacarboxylate linker and 8-connected Na₄ clusters. The resulting structure features a unique 3²,4²,8-c topology and exhibits exceptional architectural stability. Notably, HIAM-111 possesses a BET surface area of 1561 m²/g, a value that significantly exceeds those of previously reported Na-MOFs. Adsorption studies reveal that HIAM-111 is highly effective for the separation of C₂H₂/CO₂ mixtures and the single-step purification of C₂H₄ from ternary C₂H₄/C₂H₂/C₂H₆ streams, maintaining high adsorption capacities throughout. This work provides a template for the rational design of robust, highly porous alkali-metal frameworks for advanced gas separation technologies.
